content.text: Azure Lighthouse customer onboarding establishes the delegation that projects a customer's Azure resources into the managing tenant. There are three paths: deploying an ARM template directly to the customer subscription (suitable for direct agreements), publishing a Managed Service Offer in the Azure Marketplace (suitable for scaled MSP businesses), and automating with Bicep pipelines (suitable for enterprise or internal teams managing many subscriptions). Each path creates the same underlying microsoft.managedServices/registrationDefinitions and registrationAssignments resources, but differs in who performs the deployment and how.

attrs.alt: Three Lighthouse onboarding paths: ARM Template, Marketplace Offer, Bicep Pipeline

content.text: The customer always controls the delegation. They can revoke it at any time by deleting the registrationAssignment in their subscription. MSPs cannot prevent revocation — this is by design.
Public or private plan in Marketplace
Sub or RG — selected at subscribe time

The MSP authors the registration definition ARM template (covered in the Delegated Resource Management topic) and sends it to the customer. The customer deploys it against their own subscription. The customer must have Owner or User Access Administrator permissions on the target subscription.

# --- Customer side: onboard via ARM template ---
# The customer MUST be logged in to THEIR OWN tenant

az login --tenant $CUSTOMER_TENANT_ID

# Option A: deploy from a public URL (GitHub raw link to MSP's template)
az deployment sub create   --location eastus   --template-uri "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/contoso-msp/lighthouse-templates/main/lighthouse-v2.json"   --parameters mspTenantId="$MSP_TENANT_ID" mspOfferName="Contoso Managed Services v2"

# Option B: deploy from a local file (MSP emailed the template)
az deployment sub create   --location eastus   --template-file ./lighthouse-delegation.json   --parameters mspTenantId="$MSP_TENANT_ID"

# Option C: deploy via portal (ARM template → Deploy to Azure button)
# MSP generates a "Deploy to Azure" URL and puts it in the contract documentation:
# https://portal.azure.com/#create/Microsoft.Template/uri/<URL_ENCODED_RAW_TEMPLATE_URL>

# Verify delegation was created
az managedservices assignment list   --query "[].{name:name, state:properties.provisioningState,                definitionId:properties.registrationDefinitionId}" -o table

# MSP verifies customer appears in their My Customers blade
# (run in MSP tenant)
az managedservices definition list   --query "[?properties.managedByTenantId=='$MSP_TENANT_ID'].    {offer:properties.registrationDefinitionName, tenant:properties.managedByTenantId}"

For MSPs with a commercial presence in the Azure Marketplace, publishing a Managed Service offer is the most scalable onboarding path. Customers discover the offer, subscribe, and select the scope. Microsoft handles the ARM deployment on behalf of the customer during the subscription flow.

# Customer: subscribe to a Marketplace Managed Service Offer via CLI
# (Usually done in the portal; CLI path for automation)

# 1. Find the offer in the Marketplace
az marketplace offer show   --publisher contoso-msp   --offer contoso-msp-managed-services

# 2. Check available plans (public vs private)
az marketplace offer list-plans   --publisher contoso-msp   --offer contoso-msp-managed-services

# 3. Subscribe (in customer tenant) — this triggers ARM deployment of the delegation
# The --scope must match what the plan allows (sub-level vs RG-level)
az managedservices marketplace-definition list  # lists available offers MSP published

# After marketplace subscription: customer sees the offer in Service Providers blade
# MSP sees the new customer in My Customers blade within a few minutes

# Customer: check what the offer grants (read-only — authorizations are MSP-controlled)
az managedservices definition list   --query "[].{offer:properties.registrationDefinitionName,
               authorizations:length(properties.authorizations)}" -o table
Path 3: Bicep Pipeline for Bulk Onboarding

Enterprise IT teams or MSPs managing 50+ customers use Bicep pipelines to deploy delegations at scale. A parameter file lists all customer subscriptions; the pipeline iterates and deploys the same Bicep module to each. The pipeline authenticates as an MSP service principal that holds subscription Owner rights in each customer subscription (previously granted by the customer).

# --- Azure DevOps pipeline: bulk onboard all customer subscriptions ---
# The MSP SPN must have subscription Owner in each customer sub BEFORE this runs

CUSTOMER_SUBS_FILE="./customer-subscriptions.txt"
# File format: one subscription ID per line

MSP_SPN_APP_ID="<SPN_APP_ID>"
MSP_SPN_SECRET="${{ secrets.MSP_SPN_SECRET }}"

az login --service-principal   --username "$MSP_SPN_APP_ID"   --password "$MSP_SPN_SECRET"   --tenant "$MSP_TENANT_ID"

FAILED_SUBS=()

while IFS= read -r SUB; do
  [ -z "$SUB" ] && continue
  echo "Deploying Lighthouse delegation to: $SUB"

  az deployment sub create     --subscription "$SUB"     --location eastus     --template-file ./lighthouse-delegation.bicep     --parameters       mspTenantId="$MSP_TENANT_ID"       readGroupOid="$READ_GROUP_OID"       engineerGroupOid="$ENGINEER_GROUP_OID"     --name "lighthouse-$(date +%Y%m%d)"     --no-wait || FAILED_SUBS+=("$SUB")

done < "$CUSTOMER_SUBS_FILE"

# Wait for all deployments
while IFS= read -r SUB; do
  az deployment sub wait     --subscription "$SUB"     --name "lighthouse-$(date +%Y%m%d)"     --created --timeout 300 || echo "WARN: $SUB timed out"
done < "$CUSTOMER_SUBS_FILE"

echo "Failed subscriptions: ${FAILED_SUBS[@]:-none}"

# Verify all delegations active
while IFS= read -r SUB; do
  STATE=$(az managedservices assignment list --subscription "$SUB"     --query "[0].properties.provisioningState" -o tsv 2>/dev/null || echo "MISSING")
  echo "$SUB: $STATE"
done < "$CUSTOMER_SUBS_FILE"
At least one assignment with provisioningState=Succeeded
Activity log stamped with MSP tenant
az monitor activity-log list --subscription $CUST_SUB | grep HomeTenantId
Shows eligible roles for delegated scope
Customer account lacks Owner on the subscription
Customer must be Owner (not Contributor) — delegation modifies role assignments
ARM blocks Owner from Lighthouse authorizations
Remove Owner; use Contributor + specific sub-roles (see Microsoft docs for allowed roles list)
Delegation succeeds but MSP sees nothing
Assignment deployed without definition, or opposite
Verify BOTH registrationDefinitions AND registrationAssignments exist
Marketplace offer not visible to customer
Private plan not configured with customer tenant ID
Add customer tenant ID to Private Plan Audience in Partner Center
SPN not Owner in customer sub
Customer must grant the MSP SPN Owner role before the pipeline run
Definition deployed to wrong scope (sub vs RG)
Subscription-scope delegation must use targetScope = 'subscription'; RG scope uses resourceGroup
Scoping Delegations: Subscription vs Resource Group

Lighthouse delegations can be scoped to a full subscription or to individual resource groups within a subscription. Subscription-scope delegations grant access to all current and future resource groups in that subscription. RG-scope delegations are more granular but require separate delegation deployments per resource group and do not automatically cover new resource groups added later.

# --- Subscription-scope delegation (most common for full managed services) ---
# Deploy ARM template with: --subscription $CUSTOMER_SUB_ID
# All resource groups in the subscription are visible to the MSP

az deployment sub create   --subscription "$CUSTOMER_SUB_ID"   --location eastus   --template-file ./lighthouse-sub-scope.json   --parameters mspTenantId="$MSP_TENANT_ID"

# MSP can then access any RG:
az vm list --subscription "$CUSTOMER_SUB_ID" --tenant "$MSP_TENANT_ID" -o table

# --- Resource group-scope delegation (narrower, for co-managed scenarios) ---
# Customer has some RGs they want to self-manage; MSP only touches specific ones

# Deploy at RG scope (template targetScope = 'resourceGroup')
az deployment group create   --subscription "$CUSTOMER_SUB_ID"   --resource-group "prod-rg-01"   --template-file ./lighthouse-rg-scope.json   --parameters mspTenantId="$MSP_TENANT_ID"

az deployment group create   --subscription "$CUSTOMER_SUB_ID"   --resource-group "prod-rg-02"   --template-file ./lighthouse-rg-scope.json   --parameters mspTenantId="$MSP_TENANT_ID"

# MSP can only access the delegated RGs:
az resource list   --subscription "$CUSTOMER_SUB_ID"   --resource-group "prod-rg-01"   --tenant "$MSP_TENANT_ID" -o table

# List delegated resource groups (from MSP tenant)
az rest --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$CUSTOMER_SUB_ID/resourceGroups?api-version=2022-09-01"   --query "value[?managedBy!=null].{name:name, managedBy:managedBy}"   2>/dev/null || echo "NOTE: managedBy field only set for AKS/HDInsight managed RGs, not Lighthouse RG delegations"

For large enterprises with many subscriptions managed internally (not external MSPs), Azure Policy with DeployIfNotExists can enforce that Lighthouse delegations exist on all subscriptions under a management group. If a new subscription is created without the delegation, the policy automatically deploys it.

# Assign the DINE policy at management group scope
# (All new subscriptions under this MG will get the Lighthouse delegation)

MG_ID="enterprise-mg"
POLICY_DEF_ID="<CUSTOM_POLICY_DEFINITION_ID>"

az policy assignment create   --name "enforce-lighthouse-delegation"   --display-name "Enforce Lighthouse delegation on all subscriptions"   --policy "$POLICY_DEF_ID"   --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$MG_ID"   --assign-identity   --location eastus   --role Owner   --identity-scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$MG_ID"   --params '{
    "managingTenantId": {"value": "'$MSP_TENANT_ID'"},
    "mspDefinitionGuid": {"value": "abc12345-1234-1234-1234-abc123456789"},
    "authorizations": {"value": [
      {"principalId": "'$READ_GROUP_OID'", "roleDefinitionId": "acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7",
       "principalIdDisplayName": "Central IT Read"},
      {"principalId": "'$ENG_GROUP_OID'", "roleDefinitionId": "b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c",
       "principalIdDisplayName": "Central IT Engineers"}
    ]}
  }'

# Trigger remediation to deploy to existing subscriptions
az policy remediation create   --name "remediate-lighthouse-existing-subs"   --policy-assignment "enforce-lighthouse-delegation"   --management-group "$MG_ID"

# Monitor remediation progress
az policy remediation show   --name "remediate-lighthouse-existing-subs"   --management-group "$MG_ID"   --query "{state:properties.provisioningState,             succeeded:properties.deploymentSummary.successfulDeployments,             failed:properties.deploymentSummary.failedDeployments}"

When a customer relationship ends, the delegation must be removed from the customer subscription. Either side can initiate offboarding, but only the customer can fully revoke the delegation (MSPs cannot grant themselves the ability to remove their own assignment). MSPs can delete the registrationDefinition, but that does not automatically remove the assignment.

# --- Customer initiates offboarding (from customer tenant) ---
az login --tenant $CUSTOMER_TENANT_ID

# List all active assignments
az managedservices assignment list   --subscription $CUSTOMER_SUB_ID   --query "[].{name:name, def:properties.registrationDefinitionId}" -o table

# Delete assignment (removes MSP access immediately)
ASSIGNMENT_NAME=$(az managedservices assignment list   --subscription $CUSTOMER_SUB_ID   --query "[?contains(properties.registrationDefinitionId,'$MSP_OFFER_GUID')].name" -o tsv)

az managedservices assignment delete   --subscription $CUSTOMER_SUB_ID   --assignment "$ASSIGNMENT_NAME"

# Delete definition (cleans up the ARM resource)
DEFINITION_NAME=$(az managedservices definition list   --subscription $CUSTOMER_SUB_ID   --query "[?properties.managedByTenantId=='$MSP_TENANT_ID'].name" -o tsv)

az managedservices definition delete   --subscription $CUSTOMER_SUB_ID   --definition "$DEFINITION_NAME"

# Verify: MSP should no longer appear in Service Providers blade
az managedservices assignment list --subscription $CUSTOMER_SUB_ID

# --- MSP self-service offboarding (if delegation authorizations include eligible Owner) ---
# MSPs can delete their own definition (not assignment) if they hold Managed Services Registration
# Assignment Delete Role (91c1777a-f3dc-4fae-b103-61d183457e46)
# Add this role to authorizations specifically for self-service offboarding

# Confirm access is gone from MSP side (should return 403 now)
az resource list   --subscription $CUSTOMER_SUB_ID   --tenant $MSP_TENANT_ID 2>&1 | head -5

Azure Lighthouse customer onboarding creates a microsoft.managedServices delegation in the customer's subscription through three paths: ARM template (MSP sends file, customer deploys), Marketplace Offer (customer subscribes from portal, Microsoft deploys), or Bicep pipeline (MSP SPN deploys at scale). The customer must be Owner of the target subscription and must never receive the Owner role in the delegation authorizations. Post-onboarding: verify both the definition and assignment exist, confirm the MSP can list customer resources, and check that Activity Log entries carry the MSP's tenant ID. Eligible authorizations with PIM enforce JIT access for high-privilege roles, and customers can revoke any delegation instantly from the Service Providers blade.
